q: A batch job runs every 4 hours (~10GB/run). The 12pm run fails. At 4pm the next run must process ~20GB — a 2x backlog that itself risks failing and cascading into missed SLAs. What is the root cause of this fragility and the primary architectural fix?
fj: Add task retries and scale up the cluster so the bigger batch finishes.
fs: The antipattern is that batch size is coupled to downtime duration. The primary fix is to bound each trigger independent of backlog using maxBytesPerTrigger, so a 20GB backlog drains as two constant-size runs, not one fragile mega-run.

opts.t: Add task retries with exponential backoff on the Databricks Workflow; the bigger batch will eventually succeed.
opts.why: Retries help flakiness but don't bound batch size — a 2x-larger run is still a single fragile mega-batch that just gets more attempts.

opts.t: Set maxBytesPerTrigger on the Auto Loader / Structured Streaming source so each trigger is bounded regardless of backlog size, and use Trigger.AvailableNow on the schedule for batch economics.
opts.why: maxBytesPerTrigger (a soft cap per trigger) ensures the batch size is constant even when backlog grows — a 20GB backlog drains as two bounded runs, not one mega-run. Trigger.AvailableNow replaces the deprecated Trigger.Once and lets the stream drain all available data in incremental bounded triggers on a schedule, so you get streaming's checkpointing/bounding guarantees at batch cost.

opts.t: Partition the source data by hour so each run processes exactly its own window, making the 4pm run self-contained.
opts.why: Partitioning by window is a reasonable design but doesn't solve the backlog problem — the 4pm run still needs to cover the missed 12pm window, so you either skip it (data loss) or process both (back to a 2x batch).

opts.t: Decouple ingestion from transformation: append-only Bronze ingest on a tight schedule, trigger Gold transformation from a watermark/completion signal rather than wall-clock, and use MERGE on a key so a late batch self-corrects.
opts.why: This is a valuable complementary pattern — decoupling and idempotent MERGE prevent double-counting and decouple SLA timing — but it doesn't bound the ingestion batch size itself. It should be layered on top of the primary fix, not substituted for it.

why: The fragility is a coupling: batch size = f(downtime duration). Fix 1 (primary): bound the trigger with maxBytesPerTrigger / maxFilesPerTrigger so backlog drains as N constant-size micro-batches. Fix 2: use Trigger.AvailableNow + Auto Loader checkpoints so recovery is offset-driven (process everything since the last committed offset), not clock-driven. Fix 3: idempotent Delta MERGE on a key so reprocessing missed files never double-counts. Fix 4: decouple Bronze ingest from heavy downstream transforms and trigger reports off a completion watermark, not wall-clock.

code.label: Anti-pattern: clock-driven, unbounded batch
lines: # Reads ALL pending files each run —

code.label: Fix: bound each trigger, offset-driven recovery
lines: # files are done; missed files are
